subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 6 periods = 240 minutes
Total: 6 periods · 4h 0min

periods.activity_title: Listening to Nature and Meeting Jahnavi

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pre-reading: listen to sounds and share observations
Pre-reading: predict Jahnavi's dream and discuss

phases.description: Teacher invites students to close their eyes for one minute and listen to sounds around them. Students share what they heard and whether sounds seemed to 'speak' to them.
Teacher introduces Jahnavi's character sketch through shared reading of the chapter opening. Students predict her dream and discuss how she might make it come true.
Teacher reads aloud the first section of the prose up to Jahnavi's arrival at the river. Paired discussion follows on what Jahnavi's dream could be and its importance to her.
Whole-class share-out: teacher collects predictions on a quick show-of-hands and previews the river's role in the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: This opener activates sensory awareness, directly mirroring Jahnavi's habit of sitting quietly by the river. Remind students that the River is deliberately capitalised because it is given human-like qualities — a point they will formally examine in Let us learn. The next period will move into the River–Jahnavi dialogue and the comprehension questions that follow Section I.

periods.activity_title: The River Speaks: Comprehension and Close Reading

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Discuss Jahnavi's dream and the river's role
Table: Jahnavi's questions vs River's answers

phases.description: Quick recap of Period 1 predictions. Teacher leads discussion on Jahnavi's dream — why it mattered and whether the river could help.
Students work in pairs to complete the two-column table matching Jahnavi's questions with the River's answers in page 6. Teacher circulates and checks responses.
Whole-class review of completed tables; teacher highlights the River's central advice that little girls can do as much as little boys, connecting it to the story's gender-equality theme.
Teacher sets homework: read Extract 1 close-reading questions independently and attempt answers in the exercise book.

periods.teacher_notes: The table task is the pivotal comprehension move for Section II; ensure students locate the River's exact words rather than paraphrasing. Jahnavi's curiosity questions — about spiders, bamboo, the moon, and frogs — are richly described in the prose summary and can be used to deepen the discussion. Extract 1 close-reading (task_index 4) is assigned as homework since the period budget is full.

periods.activity_title: Voices of School Life and Giving Advice

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Circle words used by five school-life speakers
Pair role-play: ask for and give advice on situations

phases.description: Teacher briefly reviews homework Extract 1 answers. Students self-correct one key response before moving to listening.
Listening Task I: teacher reads or plays the transcript (p.39). Students circle words from the box as they listen.

Speaking Task II: teacher models the advice-giving structure using Situation (i). Students then work in pairs on two situations of their choice, taking turns as Jahnavi and the River. Teacher circulates and gives formative feedback on turn-taking and register.
Two pairs share one exchange with the class; teacher praises clarity and appropriate tone.

periods.teacher_notes: Listening Task II (matching statements to speakers, task_index 1 in listening) is a strong follow-up but is left for self-study given the speaking activity needs adequate pair-work time. The speaking task mirrors the River–Jahnavi advice structure from the prose — students should be encouraged to use the River's measured, reassuring tone rather than a purely casual register.
materials: Audio player or teacher read-aloud of transcript p.39

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Why River is capitalised — personalisation as device
Match definitions to sound words; fill blanks with sound words

homework.task_brief: Preposition fill-in for Jahnavi's first school day

phases.description: Teacher poses the capitalisation question: why does the author write 'River' with a capital R when it is a common noun? Students discuss in pairs using the clue about human qualities, then share.

Sound-words task: teacher reads definitions aloud; students find matching words from the prose text. The fill-in sentences about the classroom are then completed individually, connecting sound words to a relatable school context.

Teacher introduces prepositions using the school-day scenario (Task VI), demonstrating options with a short sentence on the board. Students attempt two or three blanks together as a class to establish the method.
Teacher assigns the preposition fill-in task as homework and confirms the next period will cover writing.

periods.teacher_notes: The capitalisation task anchors the whole vocabulary session — students should connect it to the literary device of personification before moving to sound words. Tasks III and IV (borrowed words from Indian languages and boats drawing) are engaging but better suited to self-study or a display board activity; flag them as optional enrichment. Task VII (prepositions vs adverbs) is not anchored and may be used as extension homework for advanced students.

periods.activity_title: Observing Nature and Exploring River Traditions

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Descriptive paragraph on observed nature with title
Class multilingual language list on chart paper

phases.description: Teacher reviews homework preposition answers quickly and provides correction. Introduction to descriptive writing: teacher explains sensory observation and models one sentence using the Ideas and Prompts table.

Students plan and draft their descriptive paragraph on nature using the prompt frame provided in the writing task. Teacher circulates, giving guided feedback on use of sensory detail and title choice.

Two or three students share their paragraphs aloud; class gives brief appreciative feedback. Teacher transitions to Beyond-text: brief whole-class discussion on why rivers in India are considered sacred and gendered.
Whole-class activity: teacher starts the multilingual language list on chart paper; each student adds their home language(s). Discussion on which language they enjoy learning in most.
Teacher previews Section B (Try Again poem) and tells students to note any animals or nature images they have seen showing persistence, as a pre-reading prompt.

periods.teacher_notes: The descriptive writing task directly connects to Jahnavi's nature-based curiosity that drives the story; encourage students to observe the same elements she notices — colours, movements, sounds. Beyond-text Tasks III and IV (poster analysis and government schemes) are rich but require more time than available; direct students to explore the poster independently and note their observations. Section A concludes here; Period 6 begins Section B.

periods.activity_title: King Bruce, the Spider, and the Art of Trying

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pre-reading: who inspires you to do your best?
Fill summary blanks with words from the poem

phases.description: Pre-reading discussion: students share who or what inspires them. Teacher connects responses to the poem's theme of perseverance and introduces King Bruce of Scotland.
Teacher reads the poem aloud with expression; students follow along. Key imagery — the silken filmy clue, the ceiling dome, steadily steadily inch by inch — is pointed out as the teacher reads.
Let us discuss I: students individually fill in the summary blanks using words from the box. Pairs compare answers before a whole-class check.
Teacher previews the next session on poetic devices and rhyme scheme; students are asked to identify one example of repetition in the poem as a take-home observation.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem's ABAB rhyme scheme and ballad-like narrative make it accessible for literary reading; draw students' attention to how the spider's nine attempts mirror King Bruce's own failures. The prose summary of the poem notes that the dominant tone shifts from quiet despair to mounting suspense and finally celebration — teachers should vary their read-aloud pace to reflect this arc. Pre-reading Task II (pictures of insects and birds) is a good visual warm-up that can be done informally if time permits.

periods.activity_title: Poetic Devices, Metaphor and Extract Analysis

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Complete sentences on narrative style and central idea
Explain the poet's use of repetition in three phrases

homework.task_brief: Identify metaphors and similes from sentences provided

phases.description: Whole-class discussion on narrative style and central idea using Let us discuss II completion sentences. Teacher draws out the difference between a lyric poem and a narrative poem.

Teacher focuses on repetition: why does the poet use 'tried and tried', 'steadily steadily', 'up up'? Students discuss in pairs and write a short explanation. Class discussion consolidates the idea that repetition enacts persistence.

Teacher introduces metaphor using the spider-as-King-Bruce comparison from the poem appreciation summary. Students begin identifying metaphors and similes from the provided sentences (Task VI.1) with a partner.
Homework: students complete the metaphor/simile identification task and attempt three transformations from Task VI.2 independently.

periods.teacher_notes: The poem's chief device is repetition — 'tried and tried', 'up, up', 'steadily, steadily' — which enacts the very persistence it celebrates; this is the conceptual anchor for today's discussion. Tasks IV (Let us discuss III — picking examples of spider's efforts and alliteration) and V (rhyme scheme and grammar labels) are valuable but are best treated as self-study or classroom extension given the time budget. Homework tasks VI.1 and VI.2 on metaphor and simile will be reviewed at the start of the next period.

periods.activity_title: The Persistent Ant: Listening and Personal Narration

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Pre-listen guesses then listen to ant story MCQs
Narrate personal experience of perseverance using prompts

phases.description: Quick review of homework metaphor/simile answers from Period 7. Teacher confirms correct identifications and clarifies one common confusion.
Listening Task I: students read the five MCQ questions and predict answers before listening. Teacher reads the ant story transcript (p.40) aloud. Students check their predictions.
Brief whole-class review of MCQ answers from the listening task. Teacher notes that the ant's story parallels the spider's and King Bruce's — persistence as a shared theme.

Speaking task: teacher models the narration structure using the seven prompts on the board. Students individually prepare a two-minute narration about a time perseverance helped them. Four to five students present; class offers one piece of appreciative feedback each.
Teacher summarises the speaking session, connecting student stories to the poem's message. Preview of vocabulary and writing work in the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: The listening task's ant story directly echoes the spider motif in the poem — make this connection explicit to help students see the chapter's thematic unity. For the speaking task, allow quieter students to speak first in a supportive pair before the full class presentation, keeping in mind the NCF guidance on inclusive participation. Listening Task II (re-listen and check) is embedded within the same period as a self-correction step rather than a separate anchored task.
materials: Audio player or teacher read-aloud of transcript p.40

periods.activity_title: Opposites, Distance Words, and a Letter of Perseverance

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write opposites of five poem words
Plan and draft letter to cousin on overcoming difficulty

phases.description: Vocabulary warm-up: teacher writes the five poem words on the board and students write opposites. Quick pair-check followed by class confirmation.
Teacher transitions to writing, reading the letter prompt aloud and modelling the opening lines on the board, highlighting transition words.
Students plan and draft their letter to their cousin using the given format and transition words. Teacher circulates and gives individual formative feedback on structure and tone.
Teacher invites one volunteer to read their opening lines. Homework note: students to complete the letter if not finished; distance-words classification (Task III) is a self-study task.

periods.teacher_notes: Vocabulary Task II (meanings of endeavour, toil, strive, braced) and Task III (distance-word classification) are substantive but exceed the period budget; flag Task II as self-study and Task III as an optional extension. The letter-writing task connects directly to the poem's message of perseverance — encourage students to draw on the poem's specific imagery (spider's nine attempts, Bruce's renewed resolve) in their letters. Task IV (tongue twister) can be a brief energiser at the start of the next session.

periods.activity_title: Helen Keller's Three Days and Listening for Inclusion

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Identify true statements about Helen Keller's three days
Select four true statements from Braille and Digital India conversation

phases.description: Section C is introduced: teacher briefly describes Helen Keller and the essay's context — a blind and deaf author imagining three days of sight. Students are invited to close their eyes for ten seconds and reflect.

Teacher reads aloud the prose summary of Section C in accessible language, pausing at each 'day' to let students absorb the imagery. Let us discuss I: students identify the true statements from the list of seven, working individually then checking with a partner.
Listening task: teacher reads the mother-son transcript (p.41–42) about Braille and Digital India. Students select four true statements from the list of seven provided in the task.

Whole-class check of listening answers; teacher connects Anuj's curiosity about assistive technology to Keller's advocacy for appreciating one's senses. Preview: next period covers writing, vocabulary, and beyond-text for Section C.

periods.teacher_notes: Section C accounts for roughly 29% of the chapter's char_count, so two periods are allocated. The true/false format in both the comprehension task and the listening task is efficient for this period's time box. Pre-reading Tasks I and II (sensory recall of a visited place) are excellent warm-up activities that can be done as a brief oral pair-share at the start if time permits, but are not formally anchored given the period is already full.
materials: Audio player or teacher read-aloud of transcript p.41–42

periods.activity_title: Valuing Your Senses: Speaking and Descriptive Writing

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Speak one minute on the sense you value most
Descriptive paragraph of a recently visited place

phases.description: Speaking task: teacher models a short one-minute response on 'the sense I value most' using the prompts on the board. Four to five students take turns; peers listen and identify which sense was chosen before the speaker reveals it.
Brief transition: teacher explains the three-part structure of a descriptive paragraph — opening hook, sensory body, concluding impression — with one sentence example for each part.

Students plan and draft their descriptive paragraph of a recently visited place using all five senses and at least one literary device (simile, alliteration, or personification). Teacher circulates and gives formative feedback.

One or two students share opening sentences; teacher acknowledges strong sensory language. Vocabulary and beyond-text tasks for Section C (modal verbs, sensory words table, Braille activity, ISL link) are recommended as self-study and a display-board project.

periods.teacher_notes: This final period closes Sections C's in-class work with speaking and writing tightly linked to Keller's central advocacy — appreciate your senses as if you might lose them. Vocabulary tasks I–IV (sensory word table, modal verbs, fill-in blanks) and beyond-text tasks I–III (Braille, International Day of Persons with Disabilities, Indian Sign Language) are rich but exceed the remaining time; direct students to explore these as a home or library project. The chapter assessment follows independently.

items.source_lo: Student identifies the main idea, sequence of events, and key characters in 'The Day the River Spoke', supporting comprehension with textual evidence from the River–Jahnavi dialogue.
items.item_stem:
Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.

Jahnavi sat by the river, weeping silently. She had watched her brother Gopi — her Ettan — leave for school every morning while she stayed behind to mind little Appu. Then one day a sleepy, murmuring voice rose from the water. 'Dear, dear! What's the matter? You really shouldn't cry, you know.' Jahnavi froze. It couldn't be the river! But the River spoke on, gently drawing out her sorrow: she had never been allowed to go to school, she was frightened it was too late, and yet she burned with curiosity — about why spiders are yellow in yellow flowers, why bamboo trees rustle, why the moon follows the same path night after night. The River, with quiet wit, told her that little girls can do as much as little boys, and advised her simply to slip into the school one morning and see what happened.

sub_items.stem: Are they trying to tell you something?

expected_elements: Student relates sounds to a personal or imaginative response.
Student connects the idea of 'speaking sounds' to the River in the story.
Student responds in complete, audible sentences.

sub_items.stem: Do you think the things around you also listen to you all day long?

expected_elements: Student gives a personal opinion with a reason.
Student draws a connection to nature as a 'listener' in the story.
Response is spontaneous and in comprehensible English.

items.question_text:
Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.

Jahnavi sat by the river, weeping silently. She had watched her brother Gopi — her Ettan — leave for school every morning while she stayed behind to mind little Appu. Then one day a sleepy, murmuring voice rose from the water. 'Dear, dear! What's the matter? You really shouldn't cry, you know.' Jahnavi froze. It couldn't be the river! But the River spoke on, gently drawing out her sorrow: she had never been allowed to go to school, she was frightened it was too late, and yet she burned with curiosity — about why spiders are yellow in yellow flowers, why bamboo trees rustle, why the moon follows the same path night after night. The River, with quiet wit, told her that little girls can do as much as little boys, and advised her simply to slip into the school one morning and see what happened.

items.source_lo: Student identifies narrative style, central idea, and key literary devices — including repetition and metaphor — in 'Try Again', interpreting the spider's journey as an extended metaphor for perseverance.
items.item_stem: Read the following questions about the poem 'Try Again' by Eliza Cook and answer them.

sub_items.stem: Who inspires you to do your best?

expected_elements: Student names a specific person, creature, or experience.
Student gives at least one reason for the choice.
Student connects the inspiration to a theme of effort or persistence.

items.question_text: Read the following questions about the poem 'Try Again' by Eliza Cook and answer them.

items.source_lo: Student identifies true and false statements about Helen Keller's three-day sight plan and explains how she accesses the world through touch, connecting her advocacy to mindful appreciation of the senses.
items.item_stem: Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details of that place and write about the following:

sub_items.stem: Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details and write: 1. Name of the place  2. The person(s) you went with  3. The things that you could: see / listen / smell / taste / touch and feel

expected_elements: Names a specific place and companion(s).
Lists at least three different sensory details (sight, sound, smell/taste/touch).
Uses descriptive adjectives for at least two senses.
Writing is organised in the three-point format given in the task.

items.question_text: Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place that you liked. Discuss the details of that place and write about the following:

items.source_lo: Student listens critically to five speakers discussing school life and identifies vocabulary and key messages about education, gender equality, and freedom of choice.
items.item_stem: I Read the words given in the box below. You will listen to five people speak about school life. As you listen, circle the words that are used by the speakers. There are two words you do not need. Words: lawyer  desks  homemaker  principal  posters  newspapers  marriage  advertisements  careers  books
items.question_text: I Read the words given in the box below. You will listen to five people speak about school life. As you listen, circle the words that are used by the speakers. There are two words you do not need. Words: lawyer  desks  homemaker  principal  posters  newspapers  marriage  advertisements  careers  books

items.source_lo: Student listens to a story about a persistent ant and answers prediction-based MCQs, connecting the ant's determination to the poem's theme of perseverance.
items.item_stem: I You will listen to a story about an ant. Read the questions given below and before you listen, guess the answers to these questions.

sub_items.stem: What was the ant known for? (i) determination  (ii) strength  (iii) wisdom

options.label: A
options.text: determination

options.label: B
options.text: strength

options.label: C
options.text: wisdom

sub_items.stem: Why was the ant unable to take the roti home? (i) It was stuck under a rock.  (ii) It was too heavy to carry.  (iii) It was pulled by other ants.

options.label: A
options.text: It was stuck under a rock.

options.label: B
options.text: It was too heavy to carry.

options.label: C
options.text: It was pulled by other ants.

sub_items.stem: What was the bird doing? (i) playing with the ant  (ii) searching for food  (iii) picking up leaves

options.label: A
options.text: playing with the ant

options.label: B
options.text: searching for food

options.label: C
options.text: picking up leaves

sub_items.stem: How did the bird make the ant feel? (i) proud  (ii) inspired  (iii) relaxed

options.label: A
options.text: proud

options.label: B
options.text: inspired

options.label: C
options.text: relaxed

sub_items.stem: How did the fellow ants help? (i) by advising the ant continuously  (ii) by standing still near the ant  (iii) by joining the ant in her efforts

options.label: A
options.text: by advising the ant continuously

options.label: B
options.text: by standing still near the ant

options.label: C
options.text: by joining the ant in her efforts

items.question_text: I You will listen to a story about an ant. Read the questions given below and before you listen, guess the answers to these questions.

items.source_lo: Student listens to a conversation about assistive technology and Braille and selects true statements, demonstrating critical listening and understanding of inclusive education.
items.item_stem: You will listen to a conversation between a mother and son. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

sub_items.stem:
Select four true statements:
1. Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more about it.
2. Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment.
3. Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers.
4. Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired.
5. Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones.
6. Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies for persons who are visually impaired.
7. Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

options.text: Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more about it.
Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment.
Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers.
Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired.
Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones.
Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies for persons who are visually impaired.
Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: The four true statements are 1, 3, 4, and 6. Statement 1 is true because Anuj expresses fascination with Braille during the conversation. Statement 3 is true because Anuj initially equates Digital India only with computer use. Statement 4 is true because the mother affirms screen readers as effective tools. Statement 6 is true because Anuj specifically asks about assistive technology effectiveness.

items.question_text: You will listen to a conversation between a mother and son. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

items.source_lo: Student uses guided conversation to ask for and give advice in paired role-play situations modelled on the Jahnavi–River dialogue, varying tone and register appropriately.
items.item_stem: I Read the sentence given below. 'Dear, dear! What's the matter? … You shouldn't cry, you know. And you really shouldn't be scared, when you have been coming here to see me every day, well, almost every day.' The River spoke to Jahnavi in a 'sleepy' voice. Work in pairs and take turns to speak the same sentence in 'other' voices. Some hints are given below: happy  surprised  angry  scared  sad  worried
items.question_text: I Read the sentence given below. 'Dear, dear! What's the matter? … You shouldn't cry, you know. And you really shouldn't be scared, when you have been coming here to see me every day, well, almost every day.' The River spoke to Jahnavi in a 'sleepy' voice. Work in pairs and take turns to speak the same sentence in 'other' voices. Some hints are given below: happy  surprised  angry  scared  sad  worried

items.source_lo: Student narrates a personal experience of perseverance using structured prompts, organising the account coherently from challenge to reflection in the spirit of the poem's message.
items.item_stem: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Your perseverance helped you. Use the following sentence prompts as a guide for your narration: 1. Introduction  2. Describe the challenge  3. Feeling disheartened  4. The source of motivation  5. Taking action  6. The result  7. Reflection.

sub_items.stem: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Use the sentence prompts as a guide.

expected_elements: Narration follows all seven prompt stages in sequence.
Student names a specific source of motivation clearly.
Language is coherent and in comprehensible English.
Reflection links personal experience to a broader lesson about perseverance.

items.question_text: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Your perseverance helped you. Use the following sentence prompts as a guide for your narration: 1. Introduction  2. Describe the challenge  3. Feeling disheartened  4. The source of motivation  5. Taking action  6. The result  7. Reflection.

items.source_lo: Student delivers a one-minute structured oral response on the sense they value most, using prompts drawn from Helen Keller's reflections to frame personal appreciation of their senses.
items.item_stem: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most. Use the given prompts to frame your response before you speak.

sub_items.stem: Speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most using the prompts: I value my sense of sight/ sound/ touch/ smell / taste the most because… / One of my favourite experiences is… / I appreciate being able to see/ hear/touch/ smell/ taste… / God forbid, if I were to lose the sense of sight/ sound/ touch/ smell / taste, it would make me feel… / I would not be able to… / I thank God that I am able to see/ hear/ touch/ smell / taste.

expected_elements: Student names one specific sense and gives a clear reason for valuing it.
Student describes a favourite sensory experience in concrete detail.
Student reflects on what life would be like without that sense.
Response uses at least three of the six given prompt frames.

items.question_text: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on any one of the senses you value the most. Use the given prompts to frame your response before you speak.

items.source_lo: Student writes a descriptive paragraph on a nature observation, using sensory details, movement, and light inspired by the natural imagery in 'The Day the River Spoke'.
items.item_stem: Observing Nature. Take some time to observe the world around you. Over the next few days, pay attention to the colours, shapes, patterns, and textures found in nature. Look closely at the plants, trees, flowers, birds, and even the sky. Make detailed notes of what you observe, focusing on textures, movements, and how light plays with these elements. Now, write a descriptive paragraph based on what you've observed. Highlight the colours, shapes, textures, and unique details that made an impression on you. Give a title to your paragraph. Use the Ideas and Prompts table as a guide.

sub_items.stem: Write a descriptive paragraph based on your observation of nature. Give a title to your paragraph. Use the prompts: Every day, I see… / I find it very interesting that… / I like… because… / I feel… as… / I want to tell you that… / My advice to all is…

expected_elements: Paragraph has a relevant title.
At least three sensory details (colour, texture, movement or light) are included.
At least two of the six given prompt frames are used.
Writing is in coherent sentences with a clear concluding impression.

items.question_text: Observing Nature. Take some time to observe the world around you. Over the next few days, pay attention to the colours, shapes, patterns, and textures found in nature. Look closely at the plants, trees, flowers, birds, and even the sky. Make detailed notes of what you observe, focusing on textures, movements, and how light plays with these elements. Now, write a descriptive paragraph based on what you've observed. Highlight the colours, shapes, textures, and unique details that made an impression on you. Give a title to your paragraph. Use the Ideas and Prompts table as a guide.

items.source_lo: Student organises a formal letter with correct format, salutation, body using transition words, and closing, advising a cousin on overcoming difficulty with reference to the poem's message of perseverance.
items.item_stem: King Bruce was inspired by the spider that kept on trying till it was successful. Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation and write them down. Now, write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Also, advise not to quit and persevere. Remember to use transition words like 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...,' to share the steps of your plan.

sub_items.stem: Write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Advise not to quit and persevere. Use transition words: 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...' Begin: '20, Rajendra Nagar / Jeevanpur / 23 August 20XX / Dear Monika, / Thank you for your letter asking me about…' End: 'Yours affectionately, / Deepa'

expected_elements: Letter uses correct format: address, date, salutation, body, closing.
At least three transition words are used to sequence steps.
Advice to not quit is stated with at least one reason or example.
Register is friendly but coherent; sentences are complete.

items.question_text: King Bruce was inspired by the spider that kept on trying till it was successful. Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation and write them down. Now, write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation. Also, advise not to quit and persevere. Remember to use transition words like 'To begin with…,' 'Next…,' 'After that…,' 'Then...,' 'Finally...,' to share the steps of your plan.

items.source_lo: Student writes a three-part descriptive paragraph of a visited place using sensory language — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — with at least one literary device, modelled on Helen Keller's sensory advocacy.
items.item_stem: A descriptive paragraph describes a person, place, object or event to create a memorable experience for the reader. Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently. Follow the guidelines given below: 1. In the opening line, begin with something interesting to capture the reader's attention. Introduce the place you visited. 2. In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and specific details that appealed to your five senses—sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch. You may include alliterations, similes, and personifications. 3. In the concluding lines, summarise the description to provide a final impression.

sub_items.stem: Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently following the three-part structure: (1) opening line to capture attention, (2) supporting lines using sensory details — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — with alliterations, similes and personifications, (3) concluding lines with a final impression.

expected_elements: Opening line is attention-grabbing and names the place.
At least four of the five senses are used with specific descriptive detail.
At least one literary device (simile, alliteration, or personification) is present.
Concluding lines provide a clear final impression of the place.

items.question_text: A descriptive paragraph describes a person, place, object or event to create a memorable experience for the reader. Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place that you visited recently. Follow the guidelines given below: 1. In the opening line, begin with something interesting to capture the reader's attention. Introduce the place you visited. 2. In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and specific details that appealed to your five senses—sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch. You may include alliterations, similes, and personifications. 3. In the concluding lines, summarise the description to provide a final impression.

items.source_lo: Student uses sound words (onomatopoeia and related vocabulary) drawn from 'The Day the River Spoke' correctly in context and explains the capitalisation of 'River' as a marker of personification.
items.item_stem: I You have learnt that naming words are called nouns and only proper nouns begin with a capital letter. Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

sub_items.stem: Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: The author capitalises 'River' because the River has been given human-like qualities — it speaks, listens, gives advice, laughs, and chuckles — making it a personalised character rather than a common noun. This literary device is called personification.

items.question_text: I You have learnt that naming words are called nouns and only proper nouns begin with a capital letter. Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

items.source_lo: Student identifies antonyms of key words from 'Try Again' and classifies distance-related vocabulary (proximity vs remoteness) drawn from the poem's spatial imagery.
items.item_stem: I Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem.

sub_items.stem: Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem: 1. up x _____________  2. fast x _____________  3. glad x _____________  4. win x _____________  5. succeed x _____________

items.question_text: I Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem.

items.source_lo: Student identifies modal verbs and their functions — possibility, necessity, polite request, ability, and moral obligation — in sentences drawn from 'Three Days to See'.
items.item_stem: I Sensory words are descriptive — they describe how we experience the world. Complete the following table with sensory words from the text in Column 2 for the five senses in Column 1. Add new words in Column 3.

sub_items.stem: Complete the table with sensory words from the text and add new words in Column 3.
sub_items.visual_stimulus:
Column 1 (Sense)|Column 2 (From text)|Column 3 (New words)
What you see|panorama|
What you hear|music|
What you smell|perfume|
What you taste|relish|
What you feel|rough|

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Acceptable answers for Column 2 (from text): see — panorama, brilliant; hear — music, rustle; smell — perfume, fragrance; taste — relish, savour; feel — rough, smooth, delicate. Column 3 answers will vary and are student-generated.

items.question_text: I Sensory words are descriptive — they describe how we experience the world. Complete the following table with sensory words from the text in Column 2 for the five senses in Column 1. Add new words in Column 3.

items.source_lo: Student engages in an interdisciplinary class project listing home languages, connecting India's multilingual identity to the themes of learning and inclusion in 'The Day the River Spoke'.
items.item_stem: I Discuss why rivers in India are considered to be sacred and most of them are referred to as female. Some rivers are referred to as male, for example, the Brahmaputra and the Sone.
items.question_text: I Discuss why rivers in India are considered to be sacred and most of them are referred to as female. Some rivers are referred to as male, for example, the Brahmaputra and the Sone.

items.source_lo: Student thinks critically about inspiration in nature by comparing a spider and an ant through observation, linking findings to the perseverance theme of 'Try Again'.
items.item_stem: I Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

sub_items.stem: Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

expected_elements: Student collects at least two folk songs in their home language.
Songs are connected to nature themes such as rain, rivers, or seasons.
Student provides a brief note on what each song means or describes.
Compiled project is presented neatly for the class display.

items.question_text: I Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

items.source_lo: Student researches how the International Day of Persons with Disabilities is observed in India and produces a written display-board entry, connecting the project to Helen Keller's advocacy for inclusive engagement.
items.item_stem: I Do you know how persons with visual challenges are able to read and write? They read through touch with the help of Braille. Study the Braille code given below and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

sub_items.stem: Study the Braille code and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

expected_elements: Student correctly encodes at least their first name in Braille dots.
Physical representation uses bindis, daal, or another tactile material.
Student includes a brief written note on what Braille is and who uses it.
Work is presented legibly and can be touched and read by a peer.

items.question_text: I Do you know how persons with visual challenges are able to read and write? They read through touch with the help of Braille. Study the Braille code given below and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.
